title: The Somerton Man — The Most Mysterious Body Ever Found
description: In December 1948, an unidentified man was found dead on Somerton Beach in Adelaide, Australia. No ID. No cause of death. A secret code in a hidden pocket. A mysterious woman. A link to a Cold War spy network. After 75 years, his identity was finally revealed — but the mystery of what happened is far from over.

segments.voice: November 30, 1948. Somerton Beach, Adelaide, South Australia. A man is found slumped against a seawall, his legs stretched out in the sand, his head resting on the stone behind him. At first, people assume he is sleeping. When they look closer, they realize he is dead. What follows is one of the most elaborate, baffling unsolved mysteries in human history.
segments.visual: Australian beach sunrise seawall sand ocean

segments.voice: The man was well-dressed — wearing a suit, a white shirt, a tie, brown shoes. He was fit, muscular, estimated to be between 40 and 45 years old. He had no wallet, no identification, no money. Every label had been carefully removed from his clothing. No one in Adelaide — or anywhere else — came forward to claim him.
segments.visual: suit jacket formal clothing labels removed mystery

segments.voice: The autopsy was deeply puzzling. The pathologist found no obvious cause of death. The man's organs showed signs consistent with poisoning — his stomach contained a third to half a pint of blood, his liver and spleen were congested — but toxicology tests of the era found nothing. The pathologist noted that whatever killed him, it was almost certainly a very rare or exotic poison, or a substance undetectable by 1948 methods.
segments.visual: forensic pathology autopsy report medical examination

segments.voice: In January 1949, detectives found a clue hidden in a secret fob pocket sewn inside the man's trousers. A tiny piece of paper, rolled up tightly. On it were two words printed from a book: Tamam Shud. Persian for 'it is ended' or 'finished'. The phrase comes from the final page of the Rubaiyat of Omar Khayyam — a 12th-century Persian poem about love, fate, and the end of life.
segments.visual: ancient book Persian text poetry mysterious paper

segments.voice: Police appealed to the public. A man came forward: he had found a copy of the Rubaiyat in the back seat of his car — unusual, since he hadn't placed it there. The page with Tamam Shud had been torn out. The book matched. And on the back cover, someone had written a series of letters in pencil — apparently a code. Five lines of seemingly random capital letters. It has never been cracked.
segments.visual: mysterious code cipher letters book back cover

segments.voice: The code read: WRGOABABD / MLIAOI / WTMTSTOSI / ATINATPAMLI / MLIABOAIAQC. Every major cryptographer and intelligence agency in the world has attempted to decode it. The NSA. GCHQ. Academic cryptanalysts. Amateur codebreakers. For 70 years, nothing. Some now believe it may not be a code at all — but a mnemonic, the first letters of words in a personal message only the writer could decipher.
segments.visual: code decryption cryptography intelligence agency analysis

segments.voice: Inside the book, investigators also found a phone number. It belonged to a woman named Jessie Thomson — later known publicly as Nurse Jo. She claimed not to know the Somerton Man. But when shown a death mask cast of his face, she reportedly went pale and nearly fainted — then quickly composed herself and maintained she had never seen him before. Police never pursued her aggressively. Investigators later wondered why.
segments.visual: woman interview detective black and white police 1940s

segments.voice: Nurse Jo had a son — Robin Thomson — who was born in 1947. When the Somerton Man's face was digitally reconstructed and analyzed decades later, genetic researchers noted remarkable physical similarities between Robin and the dead man. Same ear structure. Same chin. Same jawline. The implication was stunning: was the Somerton Man Robin's biological father?
segments.visual: facial recognition DNA comparison portrait analysis

segments.voice: The Cold War context is essential. 1948 was a pivotal year in global espionage. The Soviet atomic bomb project was accelerating. The United States had launched the Marshall Plan. Intelligence agencies were operating across the globe, planting agents, running networks, creating cover identities so thorough that even death could not unravel them. The Somerton Man's profile — physically fit, no identity, possible poisoning, a code — fit the profile of a spy or intelligence asset.
segments.visual: Cold War spy espionage 1940s intelligence documents

segments.voice: Nurse Jo, investigators later learned, had worked in the same nursing context as known figures connected to Soviet intelligence networks. Whether she was herself involved, whether the Somerton Man was a Soviet agent, a western counter-intelligence operative, or simply someone in the wrong place — no one has been able to confirm. The thread is suggestive but never definitive.
segments.visual: Soviet espionage Cold War map connections intelligence

segments.voice: The man was buried in Adelaide's West Terrace Cemetery. For years, he lay in an unmarked grave. Then a community raised funds to provide a headstone. It reads: 'Here lies the unknown man who was found at Somerton Beach on 1st December 1948.' For decades, he was the most famous unnamed person in Australian history.
segments.visual: cemetery grave headstone memorial Australia

segments.voice: In 2021, South Australia granted permission for the exhumation of the Somerton Man's remains. Investigators hoped that modern DNA technology — the same genetic genealogy methods used to solve the Golden State Killer case — could finally provide an identity. Samples were taken. The analysis began.
segments.visual: exhumation archaeology forensic grave DNA extraction

segments.voice: In May 2023, researchers from the University of Adelaide announced a breakthrough. Through genetic genealogy and DNA analysis, they had identified the man with significant confidence. His name was Carl 'Charles' Webb — a 43-year-old electrical engineer and instrument maker from Melbourne. He had been born in 1905. He had a wife and a son. He had no obvious connection to espionage.
segments.visual: university research lab DNA results announcement Australia

segments.voice: But the identification, while remarkable, solved almost nothing. It created new questions. Why had Webb's own family never reported him missing? Why was he in Adelaide? Why had all the labels been removed from his clothing? Who had left the Rubaiyat in that car? What did the code mean? What killed him? Was Jessie Thomson connected to him? The answer to 'who' had been found. The answer to 'why' and 'how' remained buried.
segments.visual: puzzle pieces mystery investigation board connections

segments.voice: Researchers investigating Webb's background found that he had had a troubled marriage. There was a suggestion that he and his wife had separated. That he had been in some kind of personal turmoil. But none of this explained the extraordinary precautions taken to conceal his identity — the removed labels, the tiny rolled-up piece of paper, the coded message. Those details felt deliberate, professional. Espionage-level deliberate.
segments.visual: personal records historical documents family records 1940s

segments.voice: The poison question remains completely open. Modern toxicology might be able to identify a substance that 1948 methods could not. But the body has now been exhumed and reinterred. Whether sufficient tissue samples were retained for future analysis — and whether any toxin would still be detectable after 75 years — is uncertain.
segments.visual: toxicology chemistry poison analysis laboratory modern

segments.voice: Professor Derek Abbott of the University of Adelaide, who led much of the modern investigation into the case, believes the Somerton Man story is not over. He points to the nurse's connection, the code, the Cold War timing, and the deliberate removal of identity markers as evidence that something far more complex than a personal tragedy occurred on Somerton Beach in 1948.
segments.visual: university professor research investigation cold case board

segments.voice: Robin Thomson — who was almost certainly Webb's son, based on the DNA evidence — died in 2009. He never knew, or never publicly acknowledged, any connection to the mysterious dead man from Somerton Beach. If he knew, he took that knowledge with him. Another layer of silence added to a case already built on silence.
segments.visual: genealogy family tree branches DNA connection

segments.voice: The code on the back of the Rubaiyat remains one of the most tantalizing unsolved puzzles in cryptographic history. If it is truly a cipher, it is an extraordinarily well-constructed one. If it is a mnemonic, the words it stands for died with the writer. Either way, it stands as a monument to a mystery that refuses to yield completely, even after 75 years.
segments.visual: cipher code manuscript encrypted message mystery letters

segments.voice: The Somerton Man case is unique in true crime history because it layers mystery upon mystery. Unidentified body. Unknown poison. Hidden message. Uncracked code. A woman who clearly knew more than she said. A Cold War backdrop. A secret pocket. A torn page from an ancient poem about endings. It reads like fiction. It is not.
segments.visual: mysterious dark ocean beach night shadows

segments.voice: Carl Webb — if the identification is correct — left Melbourne and traveled to Adelaide for reasons unknown. He died there for reasons unknown. Someone removed his identity markers. Someone left a book in a stranger's car. Someone wrote five lines of cryptic letters on the back cover. Someone knew. And for 75 years, that someone — or the truth they carried — has remained silent.
segments.visual: train station departure Melbourne 1940s era

segments.voice: The Somerton Man will be debated and researched for as long as there are people fascinated by mysteries that resist resolution. His grave in Adelaide, now with a proper headstone bearing his name, marks both a conclusion and a continuation. We know who he was. We do not know what happened to him. In the world of unsolved cases, that is both a triumph and a reminder of how much darkness remains.
segments.visual: cemetery sunset Adelaide memorial headstone peaceful
